summary:
Henry II of England (1133–1189 AD) was the founder of the Plantagenet dynasty and one of medieval England's most consequential rulers, whose legal and administrative reforms created the foundations of English common law, yet whose reign is perhaps most remembered for the murder of Archbishop Thomas Becket in Canterbury Cathedral in 1170 — one of the most dramatic church-state confrontations of the Middle Ages. Henry inherited England from his mother Empress Matilda after the civil war known as 'The Anarchy' (1135–1153), and also ruled Normandy, Anjou, Maine, and Touraine by inheritance, and Aquitaine through his marriage to Eleanor of Aquitaine, creating the vast Angevin Empire stretching from Scotland's borders to the Pyrenees that made him the most powerful ruler in western Europe.

Henry's legal reforms were transformative for English governance. He expanded royal courts at the expense of feudal and church courts, developed the system of common law writs (legal mechanisms allowing subjects to bring disputes to royal courts), institutionalized trial by jury, and established the circuit system of itinerant justices who carried royal law throughout the kingdom. His conflict with Archbishop Thomas Becket — his former chancellor and close friend whom he had appointed Archbishop of Canterbury expecting a compliant ally — centered on 'criminous clerks': whether clergy accused of crimes should be tried in royal or church courts. When Becket was murdered by four of Henry's knights who interpreted his frustrated outburst ('Will no one rid me of this turbulent priest?') as a command, the resulting scandal forced Henry to do public penance at Becket's tomb. Becket was canonized within three years. Henry's reign also saw rebellions by his own sons (backed by Eleanor) that foreshadowed the dynastic conflicts of the [[event:2235|Hundred Years' War]] era.
